title: Making decisions
instruction:
So far, your programs have been like a train on a straight track — they run the same code every single time.

But real programs need to make decisions. Is the user old enough? Did the password match? Is the score high enough to pass?

`if` is how you give your program a brain.

```python
if condition:
    # only runs when condition is True
```

Notice the colon `:` and the **4-space indentation** — Python uses that instead of curly braces.

Run this, then change `score` to 40 and run again.
successMessage: Score 85: you see the message. Score 40: nothing happens. The condition controls everything. Welcome to decisions in programming.

title: else — the backup plan
instruction:
An `if` by itself only acts when the condition is True. But what happens when it's False?

That's what `else` is for:

```python
if condition:
    # runs when True
else:
    # runs when False
```

Exactly one of the two blocks runs — never both, never neither.

Run with `score = 85` (passes). Then change to `score = 40` (fails).
successMessage: if/else — one path or the other. The condition decides. This is the foundation of every decision your program will ever make.

title: elif — more than two options
instruction:
What if passing or failing isn't enough? You want letter grades.

`elif` (short for 'else if') checks another condition if the first one was False. Python goes top to bottom and runs the **first** block that matches.

The starter has grades A and B. Add two more `elif` blocks: C for 70–79 and D for 60–69.

codeChecks.message: Add: elif score >= 70:
Add: elif score >= 60:
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: 78 correctly gives C. Python checked A (no), B (no), C (yes!) and stopped there. The rest of the chain was skipped. Clean, readable, and exactly right.

title: Comparison operators
instruction:
Conditions compare things. Here are all the comparison operators:

| Operator | Meaning |
|----------|----------|
| `==` | equal to |
| `!=` | not equal to |
| `>` | greater than |
| `<` | less than |
| `>=` | greater than or equal |
| `<=` | less than or equal |

**The classic trap:** `=` assigns a value. `==` compares two values. One `=` stores, two `==` checks.

Run this and confirm the outputs.
starter:
age = 18
print(age == 18)
print(age != 20)
print(age > 17)
print(age < 21)
print(age >= 18)
print(age <= 17)
successMessage: 18 <= 17 is False — 18 is not less than or equal to 17. All the others are True. Memorise these operators — you will use them in every single program you write.

title: Nested if — decisions inside decisions
instruction:
An `if` block can contain another `if` inside it. The inner check only runs when the outer condition is already True.

Think of it like a door with two locks. First check: are you logged in? Second check (only if yes): are you premium?

Add a nested check inside `if is_logged_in:` that also checks `is_premium`.
starter:
is_logged_in = True
is_premium = True

# TODO: if logged in, check if premium and print different messages
if is_logged_in:
    print("Welcome!")

codeChecks.message: Add a nested check: if is_premium:
Print a message that mentions premium.

successMessage: The outer if opens the door. The inner if reveals which room. This two-lock pattern is everywhere in real apps — authentication then authorisation.

title: Indentation is everything
instruction:
Python uses indentation (spaces) to define which code belongs inside which block. Not curly braces. Not semicolons. **Spaces.**

The rule: 4 spaces per level. Mix up the indentation and Python gets very confused and crashes.

This code is broken — the `print` inside `else` is not indented. Fix it.
hint: The print inside else needs exactly 4 spaces before it.

codeChecks.message: Indent the print under else by 4 spaces.

successMessage: Indentation fixed. This is THE most common error for Python beginners. Your editor usually helps — but watch for mixed tabs and spaces, which causes subtle bugs.

title: One-line if — the ternary
instruction:
Sometimes you have a simple if/else that fits on one line. Python has a shortcut:

`value = "yes" if condition else "no"`

This is called a ternary expression. Same logic, much less typing.

Rewrite the four-line if/else below as a single ternary line.
starter:
age = 20

# Rewrite this as a ternary:
# if age >= 18:
#     status = "adult"
# else:
#     status = "minor"

# TODO: write the ternary here
print(status)

codeChecks.message: Use a ternary: status = "adult" if age >= 18 else "minor"
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: One line, same result. Use ternaries when the logic is simple and fits clearly. Don't nest them — one level only. Readability beats cleverness.

title: pass — the intentional no-op
instruction:
Python requires at least one statement inside every block. If you want a block that deliberately does nothing yet, use `pass`.

It is a placeholder — it says 'I know this block needs something, I'll fill it in later'.

Add `pass` to the empty `else` block so the code runs without crashing.

codeChecks.message: Add pass to the empty else block.
Replace the TODO comment with pass.

successMessage: pass is Python's way of saying 'intentionally empty'. You will use it when writing class or function skeletons — start with pass, fill in the real code later.

title: match — Python's switch
instruction:
Python 3.10 added `match` — a clean way to handle many exact values without a long if/elif chain:

```python
match command:
    case "quit":
        ...
    case "help":
        ...
    case _:
        # catch-all (like else)
```

Add two more cases: `"pause"` and `"resume"`.
starter:
command = "pause"

match command:
    case "start":
        print("Starting...")
    case "stop":
        print("Stopping...")
    # TODO: add pause and resume cases
    case _:
        print(f"Unknown command: {command}")

codeChecks.message: Add: case "pause":
Add: case "resume":
Replace the TODO comment with your cases.

successMessage: match is cleaner than long if/elif chains for exact value matching. _ is the catch-all — it matches anything that didn't match above. Python 3.10+ only.

title: Hunt the bugs
instruction:
This program has **four** bugs. Hunt them all down:
1. `true` should be `True` (capital T)
2. `=` should be `==` in the comparison
3. `else` is missing its colon
4. The print inside else is not indented

Fix all four.
hint: True (capital), == for comparison, colon after else, 4-space indent
starter:
is_member = true

if is_member = True:
    print("Member discount applied.")
else
    print("No discount.")

codeChecks.message: Use capital True: is_member = True
Use == for comparison, not =
Add colon after else: else:

successMessage: Four bugs, all fixed! True not true, == not =, colon after else, 4-space indent. You just rapidly fixed four different kinds of errors — that is debugging skill.

title: Build an age checker
instruction:
No starter code — write this completely from scratch.

Create a variable `age`. Then write an if/elif/else chain that prints:
- `"Child"` if age < 13
- `"Teenager"` if age is 13–17
- `"Adult"` if age is 18–64
- `"Senior"` if age is 65 or above

Test it by trying a few different ages.
hint: Four cases — use if for the first, elif for the middle two, else for the last.

codeChecks.message: Create an age variable.
Use elif for the middle cases.
Handle the Child case.
Handle the Senior case.

successMessage: A real-world age classifier — this exact logic runs in every app that has age restrictions. Your program now thinks. Chapter 5 done!
